summary:
The Nile Delta and adjacent Mediterranean coastline of Egypt were inhabited during the early Holocene by communities that exploited the rich aquatic and coastal resources of this productive zone. Fishing villages and semi-permanent encampments developed along the banks of the Nile distributaries and the lagoon margins of the proto-delta, taking advantage of the enormous quantities of Nile perch, catfish, tilapia, and other fish species along with migratory waterfowl, shellfish, and aquatic plants.

The early Holocene Nile Delta communities were pre-agricultural and belong to the Late Palaeolithic and Epipalaeolithic cultural sequence of northeastern Africa. The archaeological visibility of these communities is limited by both the massive depositional changes in the delta region over the past 10,000 years — which have buried early Holocene surfaces under many meters of alluvial deposits — and the poor preservation conditions of the region. Nevertheless, survey and deep-drilling programs have recovered evidence of occupation and resource exploitation in the early Holocene delta zone. These early fishing communities represent one component of the diverse human populations of the Nile Valley that, over subsequent millennia, would gradually adopt agriculture and eventually contribute to the emergence of ancient Egyptian civilization.
